content.text: A knowledge base that powered accurate answers last quarter may be silently misleading users today. Agentic AI systems retrieve content from knowledge bases to answer questions, generate summaries, and drive decisions — stale or inaccurate content propagates errors at scale. A disciplined content review and freshness cadence keeps the knowledge base a reliable source of truth rather than a liability.
What It Is
A
knowledge base content review cadence

is a recurring, structured process for evaluating, updating, and retiring content in the knowledge base that an agentic AI system uses as its information source. It combines automated freshness signals (content age, user feedback scores, search failure rates) with scheduled human review at multiple time horizons — weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annually — to ensure the knowledge base stays accurate, complete, and well-organized.
Why It Matters

Agentic systems do not know when their context is outdated. A retrieval-augmented agent that pulls a product pricing article written 18 months ago will confidently cite stale prices to a customer. Unlike a human who might notice a document looks old, an LLM retrieves and uses content based on semantic relevance — not recency. Without a freshness cadence, content quality degrades invisibly until it causes user-facing failures.
Stale content causes confident wrong answers — harder to detect and more damaging than "I don't know."
Product changes (pricing, feature names, API versions) invalidate entire article clusters simultaneously.
Search failure rates and user feedback provide early signals of content decay if you collect and act on them.
A growing knowledge base without retirement processes accumulates zombie content — articles that still rank in search but are no longer accurate.
Benefits
Agents and users consistently get answers that reflect the current state of the product or domain.
Escalation rates drop when the knowledge base answers common questions correctly on first retrieval.
Support teams spend less time correcting misinformation that originated from stale KB content.
A freshness score gives product managers a quantitative view of KB health without reading every article.
Periodic structural reviews prevent the taxonomy from drifting into an unmaintainable tangle of orphan articles and duplicate categories.
Trade-offs
Review cycles consume KB editor time — a weekly triage that takes 4 hours must be weighed against its accuracy benefits.
Automated freshness scoring based on age alone can flag accurate articles that simply have not changed because the underlying topic has not changed.
Overly aggressive retirement of old articles removes content that users still find useful, increasing search failure rates.
Annual strategic reviews require leadership time and produce less incremental value than the continuous monitoring they complement, not replace.
How It Works
The cadence operates at four time horizons, each addressing a different type of content issue:
Weekly (low effort):

Triage new support tickets and user feedback. Fix broken links. Apply minor factual corrections. Publish hot-issue articles when a new bug or incident generates a wave of user questions. Time: 2–4 hours.
Monthly (medium effort):

Review articles flagged as stale by the freshness scoring system. Check accuracy of articles about recently changed product areas. Add articles for topics that generated unanswered searches in the past month. Archive articles that are no longer accurate and have no viable update path. Time: half a day.
Quarterly (high effort):

Run a full content audit against a checklist: accuracy, completeness, readability, taxonomy fit, duplicate detection, and coverage gaps. Involve subject matter experts (SMEs) for technical validation. Update the taxonomy structure if product areas have changed. Time: 1–2 days.
Annual (strategic):

Reset the KB strategy against current product and user goals. Evaluate tooling (does the KB platform still meet needs?). Use AI-assisted review to identify patterns across hundreds of articles that human reviewers would miss. Identify full rewrite candidates. Time: 1–2 weeks.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Knowledge base content review cadence: continuous signals feed into weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual review cycles, with a freshness scoring formula producing red/yellow/green health states

content.text: Continuous signals from user feedback, search failures, escalation rates, product changes, and age-based decay feed into each review cycle. The freshness scoring formula aggregates these signals into a 0-100 score, with green (71-100) served directly, yellow (41-70) flagged for scheduled review, and red (0-40) blocked from the KB index until updated.
When to Use It
Any agentic system that retrieves knowledge base articles to generate responses (RAG-based agents).
Customer support chatbots that reference a help center or FAQ database.
Internal knowledge bases used by employees and queried by AI assistants.
Product documentation systems where agents generate context-sensitive help.
Any knowledge base with more than 50 articles, where manual review of every article monthly becomes impractical.
How to Implement
Step 1: Collect Freshness Signals

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ArticleFreshnessSignals:
    article_id: str
    last_updated: datetime
    avg_user_rating: float           # 1-5 scale from thumbs up/down
    search_failure_rate: float       # fraction of searches that returned this but user didn't click
    escalation_correlation: float    # how often this article precedes an escalation
    page_product_area: str
    product_area_last_changed: Optional[datetime]  # from product changelog

def compute_freshness_score(signals: ArticleFreshnessSignals) -> float:
    """
    Compute a freshness score (0-100) from multiple signals.
    100 = fully fresh and accurate; 0 = definitely stale.
    """
    now = datetime.utcnow()

    # Age decay: score falls to 0 after 365 days without update
    age_days = (now - signals.last_updated).days
    age_score = max(0.0, 100.0 - age_days * (100.0 / 365))

    # User rating: 5 stars = 100, 1 star = 0
    rating_score = (signals.avg_user_rating - 1) / 4 * 100

    # Search failure penalty: high failure rate = low score
    failure_penalty = signals.search_failure_rate * 50  # max 50-point penalty

    # Product change penalty: article older than most recent product change
    product_change_penalty = 0.0
    if signals.product_area_last_changed:
        if signals.last_updated < signals.product_area_last_changed:
            product_change_penalty = 30.0  # needs review

    # Weighted combination
    raw = (
        age_score * 0.4
        + rating_score * 0.35
        - failure_penalty * 0.15
        - product_change_penalty * 0.10
    )
    return max(0.0, min(100.0, raw))

# Example
signals = ArticleFreshnessSignals(
    article_id="kb-1042",
    last_updated=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=200),
    avg_user_rating=3.8,
    search_failure_rate=0.22,
    escalation_correlation=0.05,
    page_product_area="billing",
    product_area_last_changed=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=30),
)
print(f"Freshness score: {compute_freshness_score(signals):.1f}")
Step 2: Weekly Triage Automation

from typing import List

def get_weekly_triage_items(
    articles: list[dict],
    freshness_threshold: float = 40.0,
    max_items: int = 20,
) -> List[dict]:
    """
    Return the top items for the weekly KB editor triage:
    - Red articles (freshness score below threshold)
    - New support ticket topics not yet covered by an article
    - Articles with user ratings below 2.5 in the past 7 days
    """
    triage = []

    for article in articles:
        score = article.get("freshness_score", 100.0)
        rating_7d = article.get("avg_rating_7d", 5.0)
        is_red = score < freshness_threshold
        is_poor_rating = rating_7d < 2.5
        has_broken_links = article.get("broken_link_count", 0) > 0

        if is_red or is_poor_rating or has_broken_links:
            triage.append({
                "article_id": article["id"],
                "title": article["title"],
                "freshness_score": score,
                "avg_rating_7d": rating_7d,
                "broken_links": article.get("broken_link_count", 0),
                "priority": "high" if score < 20 else "medium",
            })

    # Sort by priority and freshness score ascending
    triage.sort(key=lambda x: (x["priority"] == "medium", x["freshness_score"]))
    return triage[:max_items]

# Integrate with your KB platform's API to fetch articles with pre-computed signals
Step 3: AI-Assisted Article Review

import anthropic

def ai_review_article(
    article_content: str,
    product_changelog: str,
    article_title: str,
) -> dict:
    """
    Use Claude to identify accuracy issues in a KB article given a recent product changelog.
    Returns a structured review with specific issues and suggested corrections.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    prompt = f"""You are a knowledge base content editor reviewing a help article for accuracy.

Product changes since this article was last updated:
{product_changelog}

Article title: {article_title}

Article content:
{article_content}

Identify any specific inaccuracies caused by the product changes. For each issue:
1. Quote the exact text that is now incorrect
2. Explain what changed in the product
3. Suggest the corrected text

If the article appears accurate given the changes, say "No issues found."
Do not flag stylistic issues — only factual inaccuracies."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
        max_tokens=1000,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )

    return {
        "article_title": article_title,
        "review": response.content[0].text,
        "model": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
    }

# Run as part of the monthly review for articles in changed product areas
Examples
Example 1: Quarterly Audit Checklist (Python Script)
Example 2: Unanswered Search Detection

import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from collections import Counter

def find_unanswered_searches(
    log_group: str,
    lookback_days: int = 30,
    min_occurrences: int = 5,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Find search queries that returned results but had zero clicks —
    an indicator that the KB does not have a useful article on this topic.
    """
    client = boto3.client("logs", region_name="us-east-1")

    query = """
    fields query, result_count, clicked
    | filter event_type = 'kb_search'
    | filter clicked = false AND result_count > 0
    | stats count(*) as occurrences by query
    | sort occurrences desc
    | limit 100
    """

    start = datetime.now() - timedelta(days=lookback_days)
    end = datetime.now()

    response = client.start_query(
        logGroupName=log_group,
        startTime=int(start.timestamp()),
        endTime=int(end.timestamp()),
        queryString=query,
    )

    import time
    while True:
        result = client.get_query_results(queryId=response["queryId"])
        if result["status"] == "Complete":
            break
        time.sleep(1)

    gaps = []
    for row in result.get("results", []):
        record = {field["field"]: field["value"] for field in row}
        if int(record.get("occurrences", 0)) >= min_occurrences:
            gaps.append(record)

    return gaps

gaps = find_unanswered_searches(log_group="/prod/kb/searches")
for gap in gaps[:10]:
    print(f"Content gap: '{gap['query']}' — {gap['occurrences']} unanswered searches")
Real-World Example

Intercom, which powers customer support for many SaaS products, publishes its "Article Suggestions" feature that surfaces low-performing articles to support managers based on customer satisfaction scores. Articles rated below a threshold are flagged in the manager dashboard with the exact conversations where they failed. This signal-driven approach — rather than a calendar-based one — means review effort is concentrated where content is actually failing users, not spent uniformly across all content. This is the pattern that modern knowledge base review cadences should implement: continuous signal collection with triggered review, not just scheduled review on a clock.
Common Mistakes
Using article age as the only freshness signal — a 3-year-old article about TCP/IP may be more accurate than a 3-month-old article about a rapidly changing product feature.
Only tracking article views without tracking whether users found the article useful — a high-traffic article with poor ratings is worse than a low-traffic article with high ratings.
Treating quarterly audits as one-person tasks — accuracy checks for technical articles require SME involvement, not just KB editor judgment.
Retiring articles without first checking whether they are referenced by other articles or external documentation — broken links appear in the knowledge base and in customer-facing docs.
Failing to notify the agent's embedding index when articles change — stale vector embeddings continue returning outdated content even after the article is updated.
Building a freshness scoring system but never blocking low-scoring articles from retrieval — scores without enforcement do not improve agent response quality.
Best Practices
Assign ownership: every article should have a named owner and a named subject matter expert. Ownerless articles drift stale with no one responsible.
Gate retrieval on freshness score: configure the RAG pipeline to exclude articles with a freshness score below 40 from the retrieved context, routing to a fallback or human escalation instead.
Re-embed on every update: trigger a re-indexing pipeline whenever an article is updated so the vector store reflects the latest content.
Automate product-change notifications: subscribe the KB review system to your product changelog so it automatically flags articles in changed product areas for review.
Use AI to scale quarterly reviews: run all articles through an LLM review against the product changelog before human reviewers see them — surface only those with likely issues.
Track gap metrics: publish monthly reports showing unanswered search queries and coverage gaps to leadership — this creates accountability for content completeness, not just accuracy.
Prefer update over retire: an article that is 60% accurate is better updated than retired, because retiring removes the 60% that was correct.
Performance and Scalability

At scale (thousands of articles), the freshness scoring and review pipeline must be automated. Compute freshness scores nightly in a batch job rather than on demand. Store scores in a fast lookup store (Redis or a PostgreSQL table) that the retrieval layer queries at request time. For AI-assisted review, batch articles by product area and run the LLM review pipeline during off-peak hours to minimize cost and latency impact on the production system.
Compute freshness scores nightly in a batch job — not on every retrieval request.
Index freshness scores in the vector store's metadata filter so the RAG pipeline can exclude stale articles at the embedding retrieval step, not just at re-ranking.
Rate-limit AI-assisted reviews to avoid high API costs — run only for articles in recently changed product areas, not the full KB.
Shard the quarterly audit across the team by product area — one reviewer per area, not one reviewer for everything.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Intercom Articles
— built-in CSAT tracking and article performance reports tied to support conversation outcomes.
Zendesk Guide
— KB platform with content feedback scores and Content Cues (AI-identified stale articles).
Confluence
— internal KB platform with page views, reactions, and scheduled review date metadata.
Elastic / OpenSearch
— vector and keyword search engines that support metadata filtering on freshness score.
Anthropic API (claude-3-5-haiku)
— cost-effective model for AI-assisted article accuracy reviews at scale.
Datadog / Grafana
— dashboards for tracking KB freshness distribution, search failure rates, and escalation correlation.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Freshness Coverage
Effort
Scales to 1,000+ articles
Signal-driven cadence (this guide)
High — multiple signals
Medium (automated + human)
Yes
Manual review on calendar
Medium — misses rapid changes
High (fully manual)
No — bottleneck at scale
Age-only freshness scoring
Low — misses product-driven staleness
Low (automated)
Yes
AI-only review (no human)
High but risk of model errors
Low
Yes — but needs human spot-checks
Security and Reliability
Gate KB article publication on an approval workflow — an unapproved update to a high-traffic article can propagate inaccuracies at scale instantly.
Maintain version history for every article so any update can be rolled back if it introduces errors.
Do not expose internal KB articles (containing internal pricing, PII, or system configurations) to the retrieval index used by customer-facing agents.
Audit AI-generated review suggestions before applying them — LLM reviewers can hallucinate corrections that introduce new inaccuracies.

Monitor the freshness score distribution over time: a rising proportion of red articles is an early signal that the review process is understaffed or the product is changing too fast for the KB to keep up.
Key Takeaways
Agentic systems retrieve content based on semantic relevance, not recency — they cannot detect stale articles without a freshness gating mechanism built into the retrieval pipeline.
Freshness scoring should combine multiple signals: age, user ratings, search failure rate, and product change correlation — not age alone.
Operate four cadences: weekly triage (low effort), monthly accuracy check (medium), quarterly full audit (high), and annual strategy reset.
Gate retrieval on freshness score: exclude red articles (below 40) from the context sent to the agent and route to a human fallback.
Use AI-assisted review to scale the quarterly audit — but always have a human approve the suggested corrections before applying them.
Re-embed articles immediately after every update — stale embeddings in the vector store deliver stale content even if the article text has been corrected.
Assign named owners to every article — ownerless articles are the primary source of long-term content decay.
